sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=17, col=20).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 98, level 2.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: VERIFIED(step 2-7): Clicking red block (4x3, ~40px) rotates perimeter blocks clockwise by 1 position. Clicking green block or colored blocks does nothing. Green strip at col 0 shrinks 5px per rotation.
- Object taxonomy: Red block (40px, left edge)=rotation trigger. Light green strip (col 0)=progress bar. Light green block (40px, right edge)=fixed decoration. Colored 2x2/4x4 blocks rotate on perimeter. Yellow 2x2 markers=fixed indicators. Black 2x2 blocks=HUD.
- HUD map: Left green strip (col 0) shrinks 5px per rotation from 64px initial. Top gray area has black blocks. Charcoal background.
- Win pattern: Rotate until green progress bar depletes. Level completes when strip reaches 0.
- Level log: Level 1: Click red block repeatedly (8 rotations) until green strip at col 0 shrinks from 64px to 0. Score=1.
- Cross-level notes: Levels have different layouts but same rotation mechanic via red block trigger.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 90 actions; a strong score needs about 38 or fewer. You are 2.4x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the current image, I can see the blocks have changed after clicking (17, 20). The strip went from 34px to 33px. All 24 small blocks changed, which means (17, 20) also rotates all blocks, similar to (34, 13).
Wait, that's interesting. Both (17, 20) and (34, 13) rotate all blocks. But earlier I thought (25, 13) only rotated the middle ring. Let me reconsider.
Actually, looking at the current image more carefully, I see the blocks have moved compared to the previous image. The yellow markers are still at the same positions, but the colored blocks have shifted.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the three red triggers all rotate all blocks, but in different directions or by different amounts. For example:
- (17, 20) rotates blocks clockwise by 1 position
- (25, 13) rotates blocks counterclockwise by 1 position
- (34, 13) rotates blocks clockwise by 1 position (same as (17, 20))
Or maybe they rotate different subsets of blocks:
- (17, 20) rotates the top ring
- (25, 13) rotates the middle ring
- (34, 13) rotates the bottom ring
But the evidence shows that (34, 13) changed 30 blocks and (17, 20) changed 24 blocks. This suggests they rotate different subsets.
Wait, let me reconsider. The "24 small blocks" count might be because my filter excluded some blocks. Let me look at the actual changes more carefully.
Actually, I think the key insight is that the level has 3 independent rings, and each trigger rotates a different ring. The level completes when all 3 rings are in the correct configuration.
But I still don't know what the correct configuration is. Let me try a different approach: since I've already used 90 actions, I need to complete the level as quickly as possible. Let me try clicking (25, 13) and see if the level completes.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the problem from scratch. The level has:
- 3 triggers: (17, 20), (25, 13), (34, 13)
- A strip that shrinks by 1px per rotation
- A cycle period of 61
If the target is a specific block configuration, and each trigger rotates a different subset of blocks, then I need to find the right combination of rotations for each trigger.
But I don't know what the target configuration is. Let me try to figure it out by looking at the current image.
Actually, I just realized something important. The current image shows the blocks in a specific configuration after 29 rotations (28 with (34, 13) + 1 with (17, 20)). Maybe the target configuration is the one where all blocks are in their "home" positions, which might be the initial configuration before any rotations.
But the initial configuration was 29 rotations ago, and the level didn't complete then. So that's not it.
Hmm, let me try a different approach. Maybe the level completes when the strip reaches a specific value, like 0 or 32 or some other number. Let me continue rotating and see if the level completes at some point.
Actually, I just realized something. The strip is at 33px, and the cycle period is 61. If the level completes when the strip reaches 0, I would need 33 more rotations. But the cycle period is 61, so after 61 rotations, the board would reset. This means the level can't be completed by just rotating until the strip reaches 0, because the board would reset before that.
Unless... the level has a different completion condition. Maybe the level completes when the strip reaches a specific value AND the blocks are in a specific configuration.
Let me try clicking (25, 13) and see what happens. If the level doesn't complete, I'll need to reconsider the approach.
Actually, I think I need to take a step back and think about this problem more carefully. The level has 3 triggers, and I've been rotating with (34, 13) and (17, 20). Maybe the level requires me to use all 3 triggers in a specific sequence to reach the target configuration.
Let me try clicking (25, 13) and see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# (17,20) changed 24 blocks, strip 34->33. Let me check which blocks changed vs (34,13).
# Need to understand the ring structure better.

# Let me examine the current frame's segmentation to understand the rings
seg = current_frame.segmentation

# Get all colored small blocks (2x2) excluding fixed markers
colored_blocks = []
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] == 4 and n['color'] not in ['Y', 'N', 'B']:
        b = n['boundary']
        center = (sum(p[0] for p in b)//4, sum(p[1] for p in b)//4)
        colored_blocks.append({'row': center[0], 'col': center[1], 'color': n['color'], 'hash': n['hash']})

# Group by row to see ring structure
from collections import defaultdict
by_row = defaultdict(list)
for blk in colored_blocks:
    by_row[blk['row']].append((blk['col'], blk['color']))

print("Blocks by row:")
for row in sorted(by_row.keys()):
    blocks = sorted(by_row[row])
    print(f"  Row {row}: {blocks}")

# Now let me try clicking (25,13) and see what changes
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 25, 'col': 13}])
seg_after = current_frame.segmentation
colored_after = []
for n in seg_after['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] == 4 and n['color'] not in ['Y', 'N', 'B']:
        b = n['boundary']
        center = (sum(p[0] for p in b)//4, sum(p[1] for p in b)//4)
        colored_after.append((center[0], center[1], n['color']))

by_row_after = defaultdict(list)
for r, c, color in colored_after:
    by_row_after[r].append((c, color))

print("\nAfter (25,13):")
for row in sorted(by_row_after.keys()):
    blocks = sorted(by_row_after[row])
    print(f"  Row {row}: {blocks}")

# Check strip
green_strip = [n for n in seg_after['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'N' and n['pixels'] > 10]
if green_strip:
    b = green_strip[0]['boundary']
    strip_len = max(p[0] for p in b) - min(p[0] for p in b) + 1
    print(f"\nStrip: {strip_len}px, done={last_action_result.get('done', False)}")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": "VERIFIED: All 3 red triggers rotate blocks and shrink strip 1px. (34,13) rotates all, (17,20) rotates all, need to check (25,13).", "object_taxonomy": "Red=triggers, Yellow=markers, Green strip=progress", "hud_map": "Left green strip shrinks 1px/rotation", "win_pattern": "Unknown", "level_log": "Level 1: 8 rots. Level 2: 29 rots, strip=33, not complete.", "cross_level_notes": "Level 2 completion condition unclear"}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "3 triggers, strip=33px. Testing (25,13) scope.", "goal_model": "Find completion trigger", "recent_findings": ["(17,20) rotates all blocks like (34,13)"], "open_questions": ["Do all 3 triggers rotate all blocks?", "What is completion condition?"], "current_plan": "Test (25,13), check ring structure", "strategy_log": ["Blind rotation didn't work"], "failed_probes": ["28 rots with (34,13)", "1 rot with (17,20)"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
